notes.note: 15 March 44 BC, the assassination of Caesar; Cicero's fear here is that the deed brought emotional satisfaction but no real political restoration of the republic.
The people of Sicily, whom Cicero had defended in the Verrines and regarded as honourable clients; no distinct collective entity for the Siculi is registered, so the surface is left unbound.

Caesar had given the Sicilians the Latin right (ius Latii), a lesser franchise; Cicero thought even that excessive, and now complains that Antony, bribed, has forged a full citizenship grant in Caesar's name.
Fulvia, Antony's wife, was notorious for trafficking in political favours for cash; Cicero allows that King Deiotarus deserves his realm but objects to its being secured by bribing her.
Octavian's partisans were already hailing the young heir by his adoptive name "Caesar"; Philippus, his stepfather, pointedly refused, and Cicero followed suit, denying he could be a good citizen.

A truncated tragic quotation (the doomed house of Pelops, scene of kin-murder and curse), invoked as Cicero longs to flee somewhere untainted by such horrors; it is a literary allusion, not a reference to the intermediary Pelops of Byzantium.
